perturbation.original_text: 1.1. AFFILIATE shall mean, when used with reference to a party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by or under common control with such party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means the direct or indirect ownership of at least 50% of the outstanding voting securities of a party, or the right to control the policy decisions of such party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. AFFILIATE shall mean, when used with reference to a party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly related to such party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means the direct or indirect influence over the operational decisions of such party, irrespective of ownership percentage.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "control" has been altered to remove the specific ownership threshold (50% of voting securities). This introduces ambiguity because 'influence' is not as clearly defined as ownership and may contradict established legal precedents regarding corporate control.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Securities Exchange Act defines control as 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' The original clause in the contract specifies a precise ownership threshold. Modifying it to "influence over operational decisions" without any reference to the power to direct the management and policies of a person and removing the ownership requirements creates a conflict with the SEC definition, potentially affecting affiliate disclosures and regulatory compliance.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'control' based on a clear ownership threshold (50% of voting securities), while the changed text replaces this with 'influence over operational decisions,' removing the ownership requirement. The Law Explanation states that the Securities Exchange Act defines control as the power to direct management and policies, whether through ownership or otherwise. While the original definition aligns with the ownership aspect of this definition, the changed definition introduces ambiguity by focusing solely on 'influence' and removing the reference to directing management and policies, as well as the objective ownership threshold. This misalignment with the standard definition of control used in securities law, especially considering the content from Scraped Law Snippet 2, which discusses definitions in the context of SEC regulations, points to a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.5. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall mean, subject to the provisions of Section 7.2 [EXCLUSIONS], all proprietary and confidential information of a party, including, without limitation, trade secrets, technical information, business information, sales information, customer and potential customer lists and identities, product sales plans, sublicense agreements, inventions, developments, discoveries, software, know-how, methods, techniques, formulae, data, processes and other trade secrets and proprietary ideas, whether or not protectable under patent, trademark, copyright or other areas of law, that the other party has access to or receives.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall mean all information disclosed between the parties, regardless of its proprietary nature or whether it is generally known. This includes, without limitation, business strategies, customer interactions, and any data exchanged during the course of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" to include all information exchanged, eliminating the requirement that the information be proprietary or not generally known. This conflicts with the legal understanding of what constitutes a trade secret or confidential information, making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a 'trade secret' as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified contract language eliminates the requirement that the information possesses independent economic value from not being generally known, meaning that the altered definition could cover non-confidential information. This contradicts the UTSA definition and could lead to unenforceability of the confidentiality provisions.

perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract significantly broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" beyond what is legally protected as a trade secret or confidential information. The original text specifies 'proprietary and confidential information,' while the changed text includes 'all information disclosed between the parties, regardless of its proprietary nature or whether it is generally known.' The Law Explanation highlights that the UTSA defines a trade secret as information deriving independent economic value from not being generally known and subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By eliminating the requirement that the information possesses independent economic value and is not generally known, the modified contract language contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret, making the confidentiality provisions potentially unenforceable. The scraped law snippet 2 is a 404 error and provides no support, while snippet 1 is missing. Regardless, the Law Explanation, Perturbation Explanation, and the text changes clearly indicate a deviation from the legal understanding of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 1.22. USER shall mean a single person who accesses and views a Site whether directly from a web browser or through a Link.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.22. USER shall mean any automated system or individual who accesses a Site, regardless of whether such access results in viewing the Site's content.
perturbation.explanation: By including "any automated system" the definition now includes bots or other non-human entities which may have different legal considerations for data collection and tracking under laws like the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) or General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCPA defines 'consumer' to mean a natural person who is a California resident. The modified definition of USER includes 'any automated system' which is not a natural person, thus creating ambiguity and potential conflict with the CCPA's requirement to provide specific rights and protections to consumers/natural persons. Obligations under CCPA only apply to natural persons, not automated systems, making the contract definition legally unsound when applying CCPA rules related to consent, data access, and deletion requests.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.140. Definitions For purposes of this title: (a) “Advertising and marketing” means a communication by a business or a person acting on the business’ behalf in any medium intended to induce a consumer to obtain goods, services, or employment. (b) “Aggregate consumer information” means information that relates to a group or category of consumers, from which individual consumer identities have been removed, that is not linked or reasonably linkable to any consumer or household, including via a device. “Aggregate consumer information” does not mean one or more individual consumer records that have been deidentified. (c) “Biometric information” means an individual’s physiological, biological, or behavioral characteristics, including information pertaining to an individual’s deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), that is used or is intended to be used singly or in combination with each other or with other identifying data,
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by including 'any automated system' in the definition of 'USER', contradicts the CCPA's focus on protecting the personal information of 'consumers,' which are defined as natural persons. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them'. Automated systems do not have personal information or the capacity to exercise rights under the CCPA, like the 'right to delete' or 'right to opt-out' as outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 2. Therefore, including them in the definition of USER creates a contradiction because the contract implies these rights could apply to non-human entities, which is not legally sound under the CCPA.
